clean_story:
Walls guide footsteps. Ceilings shape thoughts.

The phrase architecture shapes behavior means buildings don’t just stand—they nudge. Like a sidewalk’s curb steering rainwater, hallways funnel crowds, staircases slow strides, and open plazas invite lingering.

A school with no corridors (just clustered classrooms) cuts bullying by 60%—fewer hidden corners mean fewer conflicts. Hospitals with single-patient rooms reduce infections by 45%; shared spaces spread germs like gossip. Even color acts: red walls in restaurants make diners eat faster (studies show 20% quicker meals), while blue calms prison inmates, dropping fights by 30%.

The mechanism? Friction and freedom. A narrow door forces patience; a bench facing forward discourages conversation. Frank Lloyd Wright’s low ceilings in the Guggenheim made visitors tilt heads upward—art demands attention. Conversely, Amsterdam’s bike lanes, wider than car lanes, turn cycling into the path of least resistance. Result: 40% of all trips are by bike.

Three related subjects are urban psychology, prison design ethics, and how light alters mood.
